trims.name: 325i
highlights: Introduced with the 2001 facelift, replacing the 323i
Same M54 engine family as the 330i in a smaller 2.5L displacement
Optional all-wheel-drive 325xi variant
Rare 6-speed SMG automated manual was available

trims.name: 330i
highlights: Top non-M engine in the E46 range
Larger front brakes than the 325i
US-market ZHP Performance Package (2003–2005) added a 6-speed manual, sharper camshafts (rated 235 hp SAE) and firmer suspension
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h

trims.name: M3
highlights: S54 engine revs to an 8,000 rpm redline with individual throttle bodies
Wider fender flares, power-dome hood and quad exhaust unique to the M3
M variable differential lock on the rear axle
Lightweight CSL version (2003) cut roughly 110 kg with a carbon-fibre roof
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h
trims.funFact: The M3's S54 straight-six won the overall International Engine of the Year award in 2001.

trims.name: 325i
highlights: Entry six-cylinder petrol of the E90 launch range
N52 engine used a lightweight magnesium-aluminium composite block
Optional all-wheel-drive 325xi variant

trims.name: 330i
highlights: Top naturally aspirated petrol E90 at launch
Replaced by the 272 hp direct-injection N53 330i with the 2007 update
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h

trims.name: 335i
trims.engine: 3.0L twin-turbocharged inline-6 (N54B30; single twin-scroll turbo N55 from 2010)
highlights: BMW's first mass-produced twin-turbocharged petrol straight-six (N54)
Full 400 Nm available from just 1,300 rpm
Switched to the single twin-scroll-turbo N55 in 2010 with identical outputs
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h

trims.name: M3
highlights: Only M3 generation powered by a V8, revving to 8,400 rpm
Carbon-fibre roof on the coupe (E92)
M variable differential lock and optional EDC adaptive dampers
Limited-run M3 GTS (2010) enlarged the V8 to 4.4L and 450 hp
trims.funFact: The E9x is the only V8-powered M3 ever made, and its S65 engine shares its architecture with the E60 M5's S85 V10.

trims.name: 320i
highlights: Volume entry petrol model of the F30 range in most markets
Same 2.0L turbo engine as the 328i in a lower state of tune
Optional xDrive all-wheel drive

trims.name: 328i
highlights: Replaced the E90's naturally aspirated straight-six with a turbo four
Full 350 Nm available from just 1,250 rpm
Renamed 330i with the new B48 engine after the 2015 facelift
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h

trims.name: 340i
highlights: Debut model for BMW's B58 turbocharged straight-six
Replaced the 335i as part of the 2015 LCI facelift
Optional xDrive all-wheel drive
Top speed electronically limited to 250 km/h

trims.name: M3 (F80)
highlights: First turbocharged M3
Carbon-fibre roof, strut brace and prop shaft cut weight versus the E90 M3
Active M Differential and optional Adaptive M suspension
Competition Package (2016) raised output to 450 hp
0–100 km/h in 4.1 s with the DCT (4.3 s manual); limited to 250 km/h
trims.funFact: From this generation the M3 name was reserved for the four-door sedan only — the two-door coupe and convertible became the M4.

trims.name: Carrera
highlights: Returned to classic round headlights after the 996
Optional PASM adaptive dampers
Optional Sport Chrono package with sharper throttle mapping

trims.name: Carrera S
highlights: 3.8L engine with 30 hp over the base Carrera
PASM adaptive suspension standard, lowered 10 mm
Larger brakes and 19-inch wheels standard
Optional carbon-ceramic PCCB brakes

trims.name: GT3
highlights: Race-derived Mezger engine revving to 8,400 rpm
Manual transmission only
First GT3 with PASM adaptive dampers and traction control
Lowered suspension and fixed rear wing

trims.name: Turbo
highlights: Standard all-wheel drive
Overboost function lifts torque to 680 Nm with Sport Chrono
Optional Tiptronic S was quicker to 100 km/h (3.7 s) than the manual
Wider rear body and dedicated intakes ahead of the rear wheels
trims.funFact: The 997 Turbo was the first production petrol car to use variable turbine geometry (VTG) turbochargers, co-developed with BorgWarner.

trims.name: Carrera
highlights: World's first 7-speed manual gearbox in a production car
100 mm longer wheelbase than the 997, with electric power steering
Aluminium-steel composite body cut weight versus the 997

trims.name: Carrera S
highlights: 400 hp from the 3.8L flat-six, 50 hp over the base 3.4L Carrera
PASM adaptive suspension standard
First Carrera offered with PDCC active roll stabilisation
Sport Chrono with PDK cuts 0–100 km/h to 4.1 s

trims.name: GT3
highlights: First GT3 sold exclusively with PDK — no manual offered
9,000 rpm redline
First GT3 with rear-axle steering
Electronically controlled rear differential lock

trims.name: Turbo S
highlights: Overboost raises torque to 750 Nm
PCCB carbon-ceramic brakes, PDCC and Sport Chrono standard
Active aerodynamics with extendable front spoiler and rear wing
PDK and all-wheel drive only — no manual offered
trims.funFact: Together with the 991 GT3, the 991 Turbo S was among the first 911s ever fitted with rear-axle steering.

trims.name: Cayman
highlights: Launched a year after the Cayman S as the entry model
Mid-engine coupe derived from the Boxster platform with a hatchback tailgate
5-speed manual standard, 6-speed manual optional

trims.name: Cayman S
highlights: Launched first, debuting at the 2005 Frankfurt Motor Show
3.4L engine pairs the Boxster-derived block with 911-style VarioCam Plus cylinder heads
6-speed manual and larger brakes standard
Facelifted 987.2 version (2009) gained direct injection and 320 hp

trims.name: Cayman R
trims.engine: 3.4L naturally aspirated flat-6 with direct injection (9A1)
highlights: 55 kg lighter than the contemporary Cayman S via aluminium doors, lightweight bucket seats and deleted air-con and radio
10 hp over the Cayman S with a 20 mm lower sport suspension
Limited-slip differential standard
Fixed rear wing instead of the retractable spoiler
trims.funFact: The Cayman R revived Porsche's 'R' badge, used on a road-going Porsche only once before — the 1967 911 R.

trims.name: AP1 (F20C)
highlights: 8,800 rpm redline with 9,000 rpm fuel cut — the high-rev icon
High X-bone monocoque frame for roadster rigidity
50:50 front/rear weight distribution
Torsen limited-slip differential standard
F1-style digital instrument cluster
trims.funFact: At launch the F20C made about 120 hp per liter — the highest specific output of any mass-production naturally aspirated engine at the time.

trims.name: AP2 (F22C1)
highlights: Stroked 2.2L F22C1 adds midrange torque (220 Nm vs 208 Nm)
Revised suspension geometry and retuned dampers to tame the AP1's snap oversteer
17-inch wheels with wider tires
Updated gearbox with carbon-fiber synchros
Stability control (VSC) added on later US model years
trims.funFact: To gain torque, the F22C1 gave up revs — the redline dropped from the AP1's 8,800 rpm to 8,000 rpm.

trims.name: Type R Touring
highlights: Dual-axis front strut suspension to minimize torque steer
Helical limited-slip differential standard
Adaptive dampers with Comfort, Sport and +R drive modes
Automatic rev-matching on the 6-speed manual
Triple-outlet center exhaust and 20-inch wheels

trims.name: Limited Edition
highlights: About 50 lb lighter via removed sound deadening, rear wiper and cargo cover
Forged BBS aluminum wheels cut 18 lb of unsprung weight
Michelin Pilot Sport Cup 2 tires
Recalibrated adaptive dampers and steering for track use
Only 600 US units, all in exclusive Phoenix Yellow
trims.funFact: The Limited Edition set a front-wheel-drive lap record of 2:23.993 at Suzuka Circuit — Honda's home Grand Prix track.

trims.name: SZ (JDM)
highlights: Lightest A80 variant — same chassis as the turbo cars
Naturally aspirated 2JZ-GE shares its block architecture with the legendary 2JZ-GTE
16-inch wheels and smaller brakes than turbo models
Top speed electronically limited to 180 km/h in Japan

trims.name: Turbo (US)
highlights: Sequential twin turbochargers for low-lag, broad boost delivery
Export-spec 2JZ-GTE with bigger injectors and uprated turbos vs JDM
17-inch wheels and larger 4-piston front brakes
Available Sport Roof removable targa panel
Top speed electronically limited to 155 mph

trims.name: RZ (JDM)
highlights: JDM flagship with the twin-turbo 2JZ-GTE
August 1997 update added VVT-i, lifting torque to 451 Nm
17-inch wheels and larger brakes than NA models
Recaro SR2 front seats fitted from 1998
Outlived the US Supra — JDM production ran until August 2002
trims.funFact: The RZ was officially rated at 280 PS only because of Japan's 'gentlemen's agreement' power cap — the same 2JZ-GTE was rated 320+ hp in export markets.

highlights: About 100 kg lighter than the 3.0 — sharper turn-in over the nose
18-inch wheels vs the 3.0's 19s
Fixed suspension and smaller single-piston front brakes
Manually adjustable seats and 4-speaker audio keep weight and price down
2021 update bumped power from 335 hp to 382 hp with a new dual-branch exhaust manifold
Adaptive Variable Sport suspension standard
Active rear sport differential
4-piston front Brembo brakes
6-speed manual added as an option for 2023

trims.name: 3.0 Premium
highlights: Identical drivetrain to the 3.0 — adds luxury, not lap time
12-speaker JBL audio and 8.8-inch navigation display
Color head-up display and wireless phone charging
Heated leather sport seats, 14-way power adjustable
trims.funFact: The A90 Supra was co-developed with the BMW Z4 and is built alongside it by Magna Steyr in Graz, Austria — the first Supra not assembled in Japan.

trims.name: GR86
highlights: New 2.4L flat-four fixes the old 86's midrange torque dip — peak torque at 3,700 rpm
Torsen limited-slip differential standard on every trim
17-inch wheels on Michelin Primacy HP tires
Nearly 18% more horsepower than the outgoing 86
6-speed automatic with paddles optional

trims.name: GR86 Premium
highlights: 18-inch black alloys on Michelin Pilot Sport 4 tires — the big grip upgrade
Large duckbill rear spoiler
Heated front seats trimmed in leather and perforated Alcantara
8-speaker audio system vs the base car's 6 speakers
trims.funFact: Despite the Toyota badge, every GR86 is built by Subaru at its Gunma plant in Japan, on the same line as the Subaru BRZ.

trims.name: 370Z
highlights: VVEL variable valve lift for sharper throttle response
Shorter wheelbase and wider track than the 350Z it replaced
Aluminum hood, doors and hatch to cut weight
18-inch wheels standard; 7-speed automatic optional

trims.name: Sport
highlights: SynchroRev Match — the world's first automatic rev-matching manual transmission
19-inch RAYS forged alloy wheels
Akebono 4-piston front / 2-piston rear sport brakes
Viscous limited-slip differential
Front chin spoiler and rear spoiler
trims.funFact: The 2009 370Z Sport introduced SynchroRev Match, the first system ever to automatically blip the throttle on downshifts in a production manual gearbox.

trims.name: NISMO
highlights: NISMO-tuned exhaust and ECU lift output to 350 hp
Stiffer springs, dampers and extra body bracing
Aggressive aero bodywork stretches the car several inches longer
Recaro seats co-developed with NISMO from the 2015 refresh
19-inch NISMO RAYS forged wheels with wider rubber
trims.funFact: The 370Z had one of the longest runs of any modern sports car — a single generation sold for 11 years (2009–2020) with the same VQ37VHR V6 throughout.

trims.name: GT-R
highlights: ATTESA E-TS torque-split AWD plus Super HICAS four-wheel steering
Official 280hp (280PS) per the Japanese gentlemen's agreement
JDM cars electronically limited to 180 km/h
Won all 29 races it entered in the Japanese Touring Car Championship (1990-1993)
trims.funFact: Australia's Wheels magazine coined the nickname 'Godzilla' for the R32 GT-R in 1989.

trims.name: GT-R NISMO
highlights: Group A homologation special - 560 built from February 1990
About 30kg lighter: ABS, rear wiper and audio deleted
Extra aero: front bumper cooling ducts, bonnet lip and rear spoiler extension
Larger turbochargers fitted to homologate race hardware
trims.funFact: Every GT-R NISMO left the factory in a single color: Gun Grey Metallic (paint code KH2).

trims.name: GT-R V-Spec
highlights: Brembo brakes front and rear
17-inch BBS wheels with 225/50R17 tires
Retuned ATTESA E-TS all-wheel-drive system
Launched 3 February 1993 - 'V' stands for Victory
trims.funFact: The V-Spec name celebrated the R32's back-to-back victories in Group A and JTC racing.

trims.name: GT-R V-Spec II
highlights: Final factory evolution of the R32 GT-R
Wider 245/45R17 tires over the V-Spec's 225s
Retains V-Spec Brembo brakes, BBS 17s and retuned ATTESA E-TS
Introduced 14 February 1994
trims.funFact: Just 1,306 V-Spec IIs were built in the R32's final year - the only change over the V-Spec was wider 245/45R17 rubber.

trims.name: GT-R
highlights: Torque up from 353Nm to 368Nm over the R32
Stiffer body shell and improved weight distribution
5-speed manual with stronger synchros
Official 280hp (280PS) under the gentlemen's agreement; JDM 180 km/h limiter
trims.funFact: Nissan promoted the R33 GT-R as the first production car to lap the Nurburgring Nordschleife in under 8 minutes.

trims.name: GT-R V-Spec
highlights: ATTESA E-TS Pro all-wheel drive with active limited-slip differential
Sportier, lower suspension than the standard GT-R
10kg heavier than the base car due to the Pro hardware
trims.funFact: UK-market R33 V-Specs officially imported in 1997 had their speed limiters reprogrammed to 250 km/h instead of the JDM 180 km/h.

trims.name: NISMO 400R
highlights: RB26 bored and stroked to 2.8L with N1-spec turbos - official 47.8kg-m (469Nm)
Openly rated at 400PS, ignoring the 280PS gentlemen's agreement
Carbon fiber aero parts and 18x10-inch NISMO LM-GT1 forged wheels with 275/35R18 tires
Upgraded brakes (Brembo with NISMO pads), intercooler and exhaust
trims.funFact: NISMO planned 100 units of the 400R, but only 44 were completed before R33 production ended in 1998.

trims.name: GT-R
highlights: First GT-R with a Getrag 6-speed manual
Torque raised to 392Nm (40.0kg-m)
5.8-inch multifunction display showing boost, temps and G-forces
Official 280hp (280PS) per the gentlemen's agreement; JDM 180 km/h limiter
trims.funFact: Though advertised at 280PS to honor the gentlemen's agreement, real R34 output was widely reported to exceed 300hp.

trims.name: GT-R V-Spec
highlights: ATTESA E-TS Pro with active rear limited-slip differential
Firmer suspension and lower ride height
Carbon fiber rear air diffuser
trims.funFact: The V-Spec outsold the standard car - 4,193 Series 1 V-Specs versus 2,709 base GT-Rs.

trims.name: GT-R V-Spec II
highlights: Carbon fiber bonnet with NACA duct
Larger rear brake rotors
Stiffer suspension than the original V-Spec
Aluminum pedals and white-faced turn lenses
trims.funFact: Nissan's official curb weight for the V-Spec II is 1,560kg with 245/40ZR18 tires - 1,855 were built for Japan.

trims.name: V-Spec II Nur
highlights: N1-based race engine with larger turbochargers and steel turbine wheels
300 km/h speedometer and gold valve covers
Named after the Nurburgring, where the GT-R was developed
Still quoted at 280hp on paper - the JDM 180 km/h limiter applied
trims.funFact: Only 718 V-Spec II Nurs were built (1,003 Nur models total with the M-Spec Nur) - the final Skyline GT-Rs before the R35.

trims.name: GT
highlights: First Mustang generation with independent rear suspension
435hp at 6,500rpm from the Gen 2 Coyote V8
Car and Driver tested 0-60mph in 4.5 seconds (manual)
Launch control and line-lock standard
trims.funFact: The 2015 GT introduced factory line-lock, which locks the front brakes so owners can do legal-ish burnouts at the drag strip.

trims.name: GT Performance Package
highlights: 2018 refresh added dual fuel injection: 460hp and a 7,400rpm redline
Six-piston Brembo front brakes, Torsen 3.73 rear axle, chassis bracing
Sub-4.0s 0-60mph claim requires the 10-speed auto and Drag Strip mode
Performance Package offered across the S550 run; figures shown for 2018+ cars
trims.funFact: Ford's launch pitch for the 2018 GT was that it out-accelerated a Porsche 911 Carrera to 60mph.

trims.name: Bullitt
highlights: 480hp via GT350 intake manifold, open airbox and 87mm throttle body
Top speed raised to 163mph (262 km/h) over the standard GT
Manual only, with a white cue-ball shift knob
Dark Highland Green paint and de-badged grille
trims.funFact: The Bullitt marked the 50th anniversary of the 1968 Steve McQueen film and debuted alongside the original movie car at Detroit 2018.

trims.name: Mach 1
highlights: 480hp at 7,000rpm; 168mph (270 km/h) top speed with the manual
Tremec 3160 6-speed from the Shelby GT350 with rev matching
GT350/GT500-derived chassis hardware, oil coolers and aero
Optional Handling Package adds Cup 2 tires and a swing spoiler with Gurney flap
MotorTrend tested 0-60mph in 4.2 seconds
trims.funFact: The 2021 Mach 1 revived the badge for the first time since 2004 and became the top non-Shelby S550 Mustang.

trims.name: Shelby GT500
highlights: Most powerful street-legal Ford ever at launch: 760hp from a hand-built supercharged 5.2L V8
2.65L Eaton supercharger nestled in the engine valley
Dual-clutch only - no manual offered
Electronically limited to 180mph (290 km/h); Ford claimed 0-60 in 3.3s, Car and Driver tested 3.4s
trims.funFact: Ford's dealer guide listed the GT500 at 4,225lb (1,916kg) - about 500lb more than a Mustang GT, nearly all of it supercharged V8 and cooling.

trims.name: GT500 Carbon Fiber Track Package
highlights: 20-inch exposed carbon fiber wheels by Carbon Revolution with Michelin Pilot Sport Cup 2 tires
Adjustable exposed-carbon GT4-style rear wing and splitter wickers
Rear-seat delete and carbon instrument panel; real-world weighings show roughly 30lb saved (Ford quotes one curb figure)
Cup 2 tires trade drag-strip launch grip for track pace - Car and Driver tested 0-60 in 3.6s vs 3.4s standard
trims.funFact: The Carbon Fiber Track Package cost $18,500 - most of it for the Carbon Revolution wheels, which weigh about 17lb each.

trims.name: RS
highlights: Ford Performance AWD with Dynamic Torque Vectoring - up to 70% of torque to the rear axle
Industry-first factory Drift Mode plus launch control
Brembo four-piston front brakes and manual-only gearbox
0-62mph in 4.7 seconds, 165mph (266 km/h) top speed
Torque note: overboost; 440Nm sustained
trims.funFact: Rally and Gymkhana star Ken Block helped develop the Mk3 RS, including its headline-grabbing Drift Mode.

trims.name: RS Limited Edition
highlights: Quaife mechanical limited-slip differential on the front axle - the standard RS's one missing piece
Nitrous Blue or Race Red only, with black roof, mirrors and rear wing
Forged 19-inch wheels in gloss black
Final-year send-off for the Mk3 RS
Torque note: overboost; 440Nm sustained
trims.funFact: Just 1,500 Limited Editions were built for North America - 1,000 for the US and 500 for Canada.

trims.name: STI
highlights: Driver Controlled Center Differential (DCCD) AWD
Brembo brakes — gold 4-piston front, 2-piston rear calipers
Intercooler water-spray system
Forged BBS wheels from 2005
Massive dual-plane rear wing and hood scoop
trims.funFact: 2004 was the first model year the STI was officially sold in the United States.

trims.name: STI Limited
highlights: Low-key trunk lip spoiler replaces the tall dual-plane wing
First-ever power glass moonroof on a WRX STI
Anthracite perforated leather, heated front seats
Black-painted Brembo calipers with white lettering
Individually numbered plaque in every car
trims.funFact: Only 800 numbered cars were built — 400 in Satin White Pearl and 400 in Urban Gray Metallic — making it the stealthiest and rarest US-market GD STI.

trims.name: STI
highlights: Multi-Mode Driver Controlled Center Differential (DCCD) AWD
Brembo brakes, 18-inch wheels with 245/40R18 summer tires
Active Torque Vectoring
Reinforced 6-speed manual only — no automatic ever offered
trims.funFact: The VA was the final STI generation: the EJ25-powered nameplate was retired after 2021 with no successor.

trims.name: STI Limited
highlights: Leather-trimmed upholstery with 8-way power driver's seat
Power moonroof
harman/kardon premium audio
Same 305hp drivetrain as the base STI
trims.funFact: The Limited's power moonroof opened one inch farther than the previous-generation STI's — a detail Subaru highlighted in the 2015 press kit.

trims.name: Type RA
highlights: Carbon fiber roof panel and pedestal rear wing
Lightweight 19-inch forged BBS wheels
Bilstein STI Sport suspension
Cold-air intake, high-flow exhaust, stronger pistons, retuned ECU
About 53 lb lighter than a standard 2018 STI
trims.funFact: RA stands for 'Record Attempt' — only 500 were built for the US market.

trims.name: S209
highlights: Larger HKS turbo with higher boost — most powerful factory STI
Wide-body fenders with 19-inch forged BBS wheels
Bilstein dampers and flexible tower braces
Brembo brakes with cross-drilled rotors
Driver-operated intercooler water-spray paddles
trims.funFact: The S209 was the first of STI's Japan-only 'S-line' specials ever sold outside Japan — all 209 cars were built for the US.

trims.name: Premium
highlights: Torsen limited-slip rear differential standard
Ultra-low boxer engine mounting for handling balance
Double-wishbone rear suspension
Lightest ZC6 configuration
Power note: 205hp with manual from 2017
trims.funFact: At launch Subaru touted the BRZ as having one of the lowest centers of gravity of any production car.

trims.name: Limited
highlights: Leather/Alcantara-trimmed heated front seats
Keyless access with push-button start
Rear deck spoiler and fog lights
Same Torsen LSD chassis as Premium
Power note: 205hp with manual from 2017
trims.funFact: The 2017 facelift gave manual-transmission Limiteds a 5hp bump to 205hp along with a stiffer chassis and retuned suspension.

trims.name: tS
highlights: STI-tuned Sachs dampers and coil springs
Brembo brakes — 4-piston front, 2-piston rear
18-inch STI wheels with Michelin Pilot Sport 4 tires
Flexible V-braces plus chassis and subframe draw stiffeners
trims.funFact: tS stands for 'tuned by STI' — the 2018 US run was capped at 500 cars, and the suspension was developed by Subaru Tecnica International.

highlights: Pop-up headlights and near 50:50 weight distribution
Double-wishbone suspension at all four corners
Optional viscous limited-slip differential
Powerplant frame ties engine to differential for crisp throttle response
trims.funFact: The NA revived the affordable roadster formula in 1989 and put the entire segment back on the map.

highlights: Larger 1.8L engine replaced the 1.6 for 1994
Added chassis bracing and bigger brakes
Optional Torsen limited-slip differential
Standard dual airbags from 1994
trims.funFact: OBD-II-era engine updates for 1996 nudged output from 128hp to 133hp.

trims.name: R Package
highlights: Bilstein dampers with stiffer springs and sway bars
Torsen limited-slip differential standard
Subtle front airdam and rear underbody spoilers
Could not be ordered with power steering, leather, or an automatic
Power note: 133hp from 1996
trims.funFact: The no-frills R Package was aimed squarely at SCCA club racers — Mazda deliberately blocked luxury options on it.

trims.name: M Edition
highlights: Exclusive annual paint: Montego Blue, Merlot, Starlight Blue, Marina Green
Tan leather interior and Torsen limited-slip differential
Year-specific wheels — chrome 7-spoke ('94), 15-inch BBS ('95), Enkei ('96)
Nardi wood shift knob and handbrake handle on later cars
Power note: 133hp for 1996–97
trims.funFact: Each M Edition got a one-year-only color and wheel combo; the 1995 Merlot Mica car with 15-inch BBS wheels was the biggest run at roughly 3,500 units.

trims.name: Sport
highlights: Lightest ND configuration — about 2,339 lb with the manual
Cloth seats and 16-inch wheels
LED headlights standard
Same engine and gearbox as the upper trims
Power note: 155hp before 2019
trims.funFact: The 2019 engine update raised the redline from 6,800 to 7,500 rpm along with the jump to 181hp.

trims.name: Club
highlights: Limited-slip differential and Bilstein dampers (manual cars)
Front shock-tower brace
Aero kit with front air dam and rear lip spoiler
Optional Brembo front brakes and forged BBS wheels
Power note: 155hp before 2019
trims.funFact: The factory Brembo/BBS/Recaro package is exclusive to manual-transmission Club models in the US.

trims.name: Grand Touring
highlights: Heated leather seats and automatic climate control
Adaptive front headlights and lane-departure warning
Navigation and premium audio
Manual GTs gained the GT-S handling package option (LSD, Bilsteins, brace) in 2019
trims.funFact: The ND MX-5 won both 2016 World Car of the Year and World Car Design of the Year — the first car to take both titles in the same year.

trims.name: Base
highlights: Sequential twin-turbo rotary — a production first
Just 2,789 lb curb weight
Driver airbag and ABS standard
Softer shocks and bushings than the R models
trims.funFact: Mazda quoted a 0–60 mph time of just 4.9 seconds for the FD at its US launch.

trims.name: Touring
highlights: Glass sunroof, fog lights and leather seats
Bose Acoustic Wave audio with CD player
Rear window wiper
Comfort-focused counterpart to the R1/R2
trims.funFact: The Touring was replaced by the PEP package for 1995 — a year in which an estimated 500 RX-7s were produced for the US.

trims.name: R1/R2
highlights: Bilstein shocks with upgraded springs
Additional engine oil cooler
Front lip and rear wing aero package
Suede-trimmed seats and Z-rated tires
No cruise control — manual transmission only
trims.funFact: The 1993 R1's suspension was so stiff that the renamed 1994 R2 was retuned slightly softer.

trims.name: Spirit R Type A
highlights: Exclusive red Recaro full bucket seats, saving about 10 kg
Cross-drilled brake rotors and Bilstein dampers
17-inch forged BBS wheels
Two-seater layout with 5-speed manual only
trims.funFact: The Spirit R was the RX-7's 2002 send-off: 1,504 cars were built, 1,044 of them two-seat Type As, and 719 wore the exclusive Titanium Grey paint.

trims.name: 2.0 TFSI
highlights: Aluminium-intensive Audi Space Frame (ASF) hybrid construction keeps weight low
Direct-injection turbo four shared with the Golf GTI Mk5
Electrically deploying rear spoiler
Replaced by a 211hp 2.0 TFSI with the 2010 update
trims.funFact: The 8J's body is roughly 69% aluminium up front with steel at the rear, deliberately balancing weight distribution.

trims.name: 3.2 quattro
highlights: Narrow-angle VR6 carried over from the Mk1 TT 3.2
quattro all-wheel drive standard on V6 models
Optional Audi magnetic ride adaptive dampers
trims.funFact: The naturally aspirated VR6 was the only non-turbo petrol engine offered in the 8J TT and was dropped after 2010 as the lighter TTS outperformed it.

trims.name: TTS
highlights: Strengthened EA113 2.0 TFSI tuned to 272hp
quattro all-wheel drive standard
Audi magnetic ride standard with 10mm lower suspension
Quad exhaust tips and TTS-specific styling
S tronic cuts 0-100 km/h to around 5.2s
trims.funFact: The TTS launched in 2008 to mark the TT's 10th anniversary, a decade after the original 8N debuted.

trims.name: TT RS
highlights: Revived Audi's turbocharged inline-5, echoing the original quattro era
Fixed rear wing and RS-specific body kit
250 km/h limiter could be optionally raised to 280 km/h
TT RS plus (2012) upped output to 360hp and 465Nm
trims.funFact: Its 2.5 TFSI five-cylinder won the International Engine of the Year award in its class nine years in a row (2010–2018).

trims.name: RS3 Sportback (pre-facelift)
highlights: Most powerful compact hatch in its class at launch
quattro drive with electro-hydraulic multi-plate clutch
250 km/h limiter optionally raised to 280 km/h
Sportback body only — no sedan pre-facelift
trims.funFact: Audi quoted 4.3s to 100 km/h, making it quicker off the line than many contemporary sports cars with twice the cylinders.

trims.name: RS3 Sportback (facelift)
highlights: All-new aluminium-block 2.5 TFSI, around 26 kg lighter than the old iron-block unit
Output raised to 400hp and 480Nm
250 km/h limiter optionally raised to 280 km/h
trims.funFact: Despite gaining 33hp over the pre-facelift car, the lighter new engine helped trim overall kerb weight.

trims.name: RS3 Sedan
highlights: First sedan body style in RS3 history
Same 400hp aluminium-block 2.5 TFSI as the facelift Sportback
Brought the RS3 to the US market for the first time
Optional 280 km/h raised limiter
trims.funFact: At launch its 2.5 TFSI was the world's most powerful series-production five-cylinder engine.

trims.name: GTI
highlights: First GTI on the lightweight MQB platform
EA888 Gen 3 with 350Nm from just 1500 rpm
Progressive-ratio steering standard
2017 facelift raised standard output to 230PS
trims.funFact: The Mk7 GTI was up to 42 kg lighter than the Mk6 it replaced thanks to the MQB platform.

trims.name: GTI Performance Pack
highlights: VAQ electronically controlled front locking differential
Larger brake discs with GTI-logo calipers
Top speed raised from 246 to 250 km/h
2017 facelift version bumped to 245PS and 370Nm
trims.funFact: The Performance Pack's VAQ diff was the first factory locking front differential ever fitted to a GTI.

trims.name: GTI Clubsport
highlights: Overboost function briefly lifts output to 290PS
Clubsport-specific front splitter and rear wing add real downforce
VAQ locking front differential standard
Built to celebrate 40 years of the GTI
trims.funFact: Sold in the UK as the 'GTI Clubsport Edition 40' to mark the GTI's 40th anniversary in 2016.

trims.name: GTI Clubsport S
highlights: Rear seats deleted and sound insulation reduced to save weight
Derestricted 265 km/h top speed
Limited to 400 units worldwide
Special Nürburgring suspension mode in the driving profile selector
trims.funFact: In May 2016 it lapped the Nürburgring Nordschleife in 7:49.21 with Benny Leuchter driving, a new record for front-wheel-drive production cars.

trims.name: Golf R (pre-facelift)
highlights: 4MOTION all-wheel drive with fifth-generation Haldex coupling
DSG version cuts 0-100 km/h to 4.9s
Shares its 300PS EA888 tune with the Audi S3 8V
Quad exhaust and R-specific bumpers
trims.funFact: At launch it was the most powerful production Golf VW had ever built.

trims.name: Golf R (facelift)
highlights: Power up from 300 to 310PS, torque up to 400Nm
New 7-speed DSG replaced the old 6-speed unit
0-100 km/h in 4.6s with DSG
Optional Akrapovic titanium exhaust offered from the factory
trims.funFact: The optional Performance package removed the 250 km/h limiter, lifting top speed to around 267 km/h (166 mph).

trims.name: C63
highlights: Hand-built M177 V8 with turbos mounted inside the V
Mechanical limited-slip rear differential
Three-stage AMG Ride Control adaptive suspension
2018 facelift brought the 9-speed MCT and AMG Dynamics modes
trims.funFact: Each M177 engine is assembled by a single technician in Affalterbach under AMG's 'one man, one engine' principle.

trims.name: C63 S
highlights: Electronically controlled limited-slip rear differential
Dynamic engine and transmission mounts
Higher boost pressure lifts output to 510hp and 700Nm
AMG Driver's Package raises the limiter to 290 km/h
trims.funFact: The M177's 'hot inside V' layout places both turbochargers between the cylinder banks and is closely related to the M178 in the AMG GT.

trims.name: A45 AMG (pre-facelift)
highlights: World's most powerful series-production 2.0L four-cylinder at launch
4MATIC all-wheel drive with electro-hydraulically controlled rear clutch
Hand-built M133 engine with twin-scroll turbocharger
AMG's first transverse-engined compact performance car
trims.funFact: Its 181hp-per-litre specific output beat contemporary supercars at launch in 2013.

trims.name: A45 (facelift)
highlights: Output raised to 381hp and 475Nm
0-100 km/h cut from 4.6s to 4.2s
Shorter ratios and faster shifts for the 7-speed DCT
Optional AMG Dynamic Plus package with mechanical front axle locking differential
trims.funFact: At launch Mercedes billed the facelifted A45 as the world's most powerful compact sports model, with a specific output of roughly 191hp per litre.
